retrieved_docs.title: Strangulation Injuries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Strangulation injuries encompass a diverse range of traumatic pathology resulting from mechanical force applied externally to the neck and surrounding structures. As a form of asphyxia, these injuries can impair cerebral oxygen delivery through compression of cervical blood vessels or tracheal occlusion. Death occurs rapidly unless the compressing force is relieved. Relying solely on external signs may lead to missed diagnoses of underlying damage. While “choking” is often used colloquially to describe strangulation, the term more accurately refers to internal or partial occlusion of the proximal airway in medical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Arterial Occlusion(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most common cause of acute limb ischemia is in situ thrombotic occlusion. It is more common in the lower extremities, and the initiating event is a preexisting history of peripheral artery disease (PAD). Thrombotic occlusions can occur in the upper and lower extremities, but most commonly affect the superficial femoral artery. Patients with a preexisting PAD history tend to have well-established collateral vessels, creating variability in symptoms and severity. Other causes include embolic occlusion from the left heart, aorta, and iliac vessels, and penetrating or blunt trauma. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Extrusive Luxation Injuries
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment decisions are based on the degree of displacement, mobility, occlusal interferences, root formation, and the child's cooperation. If the tooth is not interfering with the occlusion, leave it to reposition spontaneously. If the tooth is excessively mobile or extruded >3 mm, extract it under local anesthesia.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: There are many ways to examine the quality of occlusion, and this is exemplified when considering dental implants or denture design. In maxillofacial trauma, occlusion can be altered from the trauma itself or suboptimal management of fractures. Keeping the dental occlusion as the foundation for these repairs prevents long-term malocclusion. [3] In other cases, malocclusion can be developmental and adversely impact cosmesis, which is the case for some patients seeking orthognathic surgery. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Strangulation Injuries -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Vascular trauma in strangulation injuries can range from intimal tears to complete vessel transection. These injuries may lead to long-term complications, including vascular abnormalities such as pseudoaneurysms. Lower pressures are sufficient to occlude cervical veins, whereas higher pressures are required to deform or injure cervical arteries. Thrombotic events may occur, potentially resulting in acute occlusion or embolic complications. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Fracture -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: While it is often difficult to determine the patient’s preoperative dental occlusion status, it is helpful if dental records are available to serve as a guideline for surgical reduction of the fracture or fractures. The system most commonly used to characterize dental occlusion is the Angle classification. In class I occlusion, the mesiobuccal cusp of the first maxillary molar fits into the buccal groove of the first mandibular molar. In class II occlusion – an overbite – the mesiobuccal cusp of the first maxillary molar sits mesial (anterior) to the buccal groove of the first mandibular molar. In class III occlusion – an underbite – the mesiobuccal cusp of the first maxillary molar sits distal (posterior) to the buccal groove of the first mandibular molar. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Treatment of Crush Injury and Compartment Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: ACS has both traumatic and nontraumatic etiologies. Long bone fractures, especially comminuted fractures, account for the highest proportion of ACS cases. Most ACS syndromes arise from a severe leg injury. In 1 study, 414 acute tibial fractures were evaluated. Mid-shaft tibial fractures had the highest rate of compartment syndrome (10%) and accounted for 40% of all trauma-related ACS. Trauma without fracture can also cause ACS. Examples include crush injury, severe thermal burns, overly constrictive bandages, penetrating trauma, and damage to vascular structures. Nontraumatic causes include vascular occlusion, embolus, ischemia-reperfusion injury, bleeding disorders, vascular disease, nephrotic syndrome, envenomations and bites, extravasation of intravenous fluids, injection of recreational drugs, and prolonged limb compression from tight casts or tourniquets. Younger patients account for the majority of cases. [3] [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Mobility of the mandible and/or the maxilla can create complex clinical situations in which occlusion can be altered adversely. Maxillomandibular fixation (MMF) is used intraoperatively to re-establish a patient’s occlusion. This can be achieved using Erich arch bars, inter-maxillary fixation screws, or rapid maxillomandibular fixation systems, among other techniques. With native occlusion secured using MMF, the midfacial fractures and/or mandibular fractures can be reduced and internally fixated. [16] [21] In this way, the occlusion is the foundation of the repair.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The child's cooperation in an emergency, the time for natural exfoliation of the tooth, and the impact on the occlusion are all important considerations that influence treatment. A conservative approach involving observation is often the most appropriate option. [20] But, extracting the traumatized tooth is usually required in cases of tooth fracture with pulp involvement, luxation injuries close to the developing permanent tooth, and those that interfere with occlusion. [14] At the initial presentation, a rapid referral should be made to a child-orientated team with expertise in managing TDIs.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Lateral Luxation Injuries
retrieved_docs.content: If there is minimal or no occlusal interference, the tooth should be allowed to reposition spontaneously, usually within six months. If the tooth is severely displaced and there is a risk of ingestion or aspiration, the tooth should be extracted under local anesthesia.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Body Fracture -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Non-displaced favorable fractures Presence of healthy dentition with sufficient teeth to obtain a stable occlusion Grossly comminuted fractures Edentulous fractures Fractures in children with developing dentition Presence of adequate occlusion Good facial esthetics and adequate open mouth

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Traumatic Ischemia Hyperbaric Evaluation and Treatment -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Multi-place chambers are steel with acrylic viewing ports and can fit 2 to more than 20 people. They are pressurized with air while the patients breathe oxygen through a tight-fitting mask or a special hood sealed around the neck with a rubber neck dam. For any potential air breaks, the patients breathe the chamber air. These multi-place chambers are often large complexes with large compressors and reserve air and oxygen tanks.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Arterial Occlusion(Archived) -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The following is a list of complications that patients can develop as a result of acute arterial occlusion: Compartment syndrome [19] Amputation Necrosis and gangrene Bleeding Stroke Myocardial infarction Death

retrieved_docs.title: Human Bites -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Unlike other animal bites, human bites are commonly acquired as a closed-fist injury where one individual punches another in the face and lacerates their hand on the other’s teeth. Occlusion bites have the same risk profile as closed-fist injuries but are more easily recognized and have less prevalence of skin penetration over areas with structures directly below the skin. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Trauma -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Minor injuries are typically treated with nonoperative management. Moderate injuries are given a pelvic binder in the field, and then should be considered for angioembolization if a blush is seen on CT scan. Severe injuries require more intense intervention. A pelvic binder should be placed in the field. Upon arrival, the patient should be treated with preperitoneal packing, resuscitative endovascular balloon occlusion of the aorta (REBOA), or angioembolization. [4] These treatment options will be discussed in more detail below.

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Trauma -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Potential REBOA complications are numerous and mainly involve vessel injuries. The balloon can cause an arterial rupture, perforation, or dissection. The catheter itself can create lower extremity ischemia and subsequent reperfusion injury and compartment syndrome. Balloon occlusion also leads to distal ischemia. Prolonged occlusion leads to organ ischemia, which can be irreversible. Organ reperfusion can lead to multiorgan dysfunction or failure, including acute kidney injury, liver failure, intestinal ischemia, spinal cord infarction, and death. Access site complications can be treated with patch repairs, arterial reconstructions with native or artificial tissue, or bypasses. If these measures fail, then the limb would require amputation. Aortic injuries from REBOA are generally life or limb-threatening. Multiorgan dysfunction is generally treated medically. [35] [36]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2082
retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injuries or internal injuries are associated with open wounds (جرح مفتوح), indicating that such traumatic forces often result in visible tissue disruption.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” is associated with crushing injury or internal injury, indicating that these types of injuries are frequently described as occurring because of specific causative events. This association highlights the importance of identifying the underlying mechanisms when evaluating patients with crushing or internal injuries, as the wording “due to” often signals a direct link between the injury and its precipitating factor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2027
retrieved_docs.content: Open wounds (جرح مفتوح) are clinically associated with crushing injuries or internal injuries, indicating that such traumatic mechanisms often result in an open wound presentation. Within the category of open wounds, cut wounds (جرح قطع) represent a specific subtype, meaning that a cut wound is a particular form of open wound. Recognizing that crushing or internal injuries can lead to open wounds, and that cut wounds are a distinct subset of these, aids clinicians in diagnosing the nature of the trauma and selecting appropriate management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1773
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery rupture is closely linked to the condition described as احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ, which represents an acute injury to the heart muscle. This acute myocardial event is closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, انسداد الشريان التاجي (coronary artery occlusion). In addition, احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ is mapped to انصمام الشريان التاجي (coronary artery embolism) and خثار الشريان التاجي (coronary artery thrombosis). Together, these concepts describe a spectrum of pathophysiological mechanisms—rupture, embolism, thrombosis, and occlusion—that can precipitate an acute myocardial infarction. Recognizing the interrelationship among coronary artery rupture, occlusion, embolism, and thrombosis is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and timely therapeutic intervention in patients presenting with acute cardiac injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1781
retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injury or internal injury is associated with the medical condition referred to as إصابة (injury), indicating that such traumatic forces commonly result in injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1186
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of chest is categorized as a type of crushing injury or internal injury, indicating that it has the broader parent classification of crushing injury or internal injury. As a traumatic event, an internal injury of chest can act as a cause of subsequent medical complications. The occurrence of this chest injury is frequently described as being “due to” and therefore is associated with crushing mechanisms or other internal injury processes. Understanding that internal injury of chest is both a traumatic event that can be a cause of further injury and a subcategory within crushing injury or internal injury helps clinicians assess its etiology, anticipate related complications, and guide appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 975
retrieved_docs.content: artery, intermediate artery). Despite these variations in presentation and vascular involvement, the pathological finding site for all of them is the myocardial tissue, underscoring that myocardial injury is the central feature of acute coronary syndromes regardless of the precise ECG morphology or culprit vessel. Recognizing the specific type of infarction and its associated arterial occlusion aids clinicians in tailoring reperfusion strategies and optimizing patient care while acknowledging that the underlying myocardial damage remains the pivotal concern.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 797
retrieved_docs.content: particular bones) are nested under a universal category of late effects, which also encompasses non‑fracture injuries such as burns and frostbite. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in systematically assessing and managing the long‑term sequelae of diverse traumatic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2824
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery rupture is mapped to the condition expressed by the Arabic term احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ, which denotes an acute injury of the heart muscle. This acute cardiac condition is in turn mapped to انصمام الشريان التاجي, indicating that coronary artery thrombosis is a related pathological process. Together, these relationships suggest that a rupture of a coronary artery can precipitate an acute myocardial event that is closely linked with thrombotic occlusion of the coronary artery, underscoring the interconnected nature of vascular injury, acute myocardial damage, and thrombosis in cardiac care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1565
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of the chest is classified as a type of crushing injury or internal injury, placing it within the broader category of internal injuries. As a traumatic event, an internal chest injury can serve as a cause of subsequent medical complications, highlighting its significance in the cascade of injury-related pathophysiology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2187
retrieved_docs.content: Accident caused by firearm missile is identified as a specific type of accidental physical contact event and is directly associated with injury by a bullet (إصابة بعيار ناري). This classification highlights that firearm missile accidents represent a subset of accidental physical contact traumas, with the principal clinical manifestation being a bullet wound, which is essential for accurate injury coding, epidemiologic tracking, and appropriate emergency medical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1057
retrieved_docs.content: grouped under a unified late‑effect classification that is further subdivided by anatomical region. This structured relationship aids clinicians in linking acute injury diagnoses with their potential long‑term sequelae and facilitates comprehensive patient management across different injury types and body regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2695
retrieved_docs.content: Unspecified site injury is a classification that primarily maps from the broader concept of multiple injuries (إصابات متعددة), indicating that when an injury involves several locations or the exact site cannot be determined, it is derived from the category of multiple injuries. This relationship helps clinicians and coders group injuries lacking a specific anatomical site under a unified descriptor that reflects the presence of multiple traumatic events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 152
retrieved_docs.content: post‑procedural monitoring. Coronary artery rupture (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ) is synonymous with coronary artery occlusion (انسداد الشريان التاجي), embolism (انصمام الشريان التاجي) and thrombosis (خثار الشريان التاجي), linking mechanical injury to acute myocardial infarction. Injection of the coronary artery, a specific form of heart injection, is related to coronary thrombolysis and is also encompassed by coronary artery surgery. Coronary thrombolysis is classified as a distinct therapeutic modality performed indirectly at the coronary arteries; it has an inverse‑ISA relationship with interventional cardiac catheterization and serves as a parent process to the removal of coronary artery obstruction, indicating that pharmacologic clot dissolution complements mechanical removal. Coronary stenosis (strictuur; coronair), described as narrowing (التَضَيُّق), occurs within the coronary arteries and is associated with a spectrum of arterial disorders: coronary arterial blood supply

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2819
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ) is closely related and may be considered synonymous with coronary artery occlusion (انسداد الشريان التاجي). It is also mapped to coronary artery embolism (انصمام الشريان التاجي), indicating that these terms describe similar pathological events in which a blockage of a coronary artery—whether by occlusion or embolic material—leads to sudden injury of the heart muscle. Recognizing the interchangeability of these concepts assists clinicians in diagnosing and managing the acute coronary syndrome that results from compromised coronary blood flow.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” is associated with crushing injury or internal injury, and crushing injury or internal injury is in turn associated with the Arabic term “إصابة,” which means injury; thus, when a condition is described as “due to” a crushing or internal injury, it denotes a direct causal link to an injury.
